annotations.label: Within an order of magnitude

anchor.snippet: the ratio of the greater to the lesser is between 1 and 10

annotations.note: An informal proximity notion (ratio between 1 and 10) with no corresponding named definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numbers within an order of magnitude

anchor.snippet: 1 and 1.02 are within an order of magnitude

annotations.note: A concrete numerical illustration, not a formalizable statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude as factor of ten

anchor.snippet: Each division or multiplication by 10 is called an order of magnitude

annotations.note: An informal characterization of orders of magnitude as factors of ten, with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Orders of magnitude between numbers

anchor.snippet: there is one order of magnitude between 2 and 20

annotations.note: A numerical example with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude (smallest power of 10)

anchor.snippet: the order of magnitude of a number is the smallest power of 10 used to represent that number

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Int.log` (greatest power of b with bⁿ ≤ r) generalizes the truncated-power-of-ten idea, but 'order of magnitude' is not itself defined.

annotations.label: Geometric halfway point

anchor.snippet: represents a geometric halfway point within the range of possible values

annotations.note: An informal interpretive remark about geometric means of decade ranges, not formalized.

annotations.label: Simpler definition

anchor.snippet: Some use a simpler definition where

annotations.note: An alternative informal convention for order of magnitude, with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: One order of magnitude difference

anchor.snippet: If numbers differ by one order of magnitude, one number is about 10 times larger than the other

annotations.note: An informal approximate statement about decade differences, not formalized.

annotations.label: Two orders of magnitude difference

anchor.snippet: If values differ by two orders of magnitude, they differ by a factor of about 100

annotations.note: An informal approximate statement about two-decade differences, not formalized.

annotations.label: Same order of magnitude

anchor.snippet: Two numbers of the same order of magnitude have roughly the same scale

annotations.note: An informal equivalence notion with no corresponding Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude by truncation

anchor.section: Calculating the order of magnitude by truncation
anchor.snippet: the order of magnitude of a number can be defined in terms of the common logarithm

annotations.note: `Int.log` is exactly the floor of the base-b logarithm, generalizing the truncated common-logarithm definition but not naming 'order of magnitude'.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude of 4,000,000

anchor.section: Calculating the order of magnitude by truncation
anchor.snippet: the number 4 000 000 has a logarithm (in base 10) of 6.602; its order of magnitude is 6

annotations.note: A specific numerical computation, not a formalized statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Seven-figure income

anchor.section: Calculating the order of magnitude by truncation
anchor.snippet: the order of magnitude is the number of figures minus one

annotations.note: `Nat.length_digits` proves digit count = log b n + 1, which generalizes 'order of magnitude = figures − 1' for any base.

annotations.label: Order-of-magnitude estimate

anchor.snippet: is an estimate rounded to the nearest power of ten

annotations.note: Mathlib has `round` and `Real.logb` but no combined named definition of an order-of-magnitude estimate.

annotations.label: Earth population estimate

anchor.snippet: for a variable between about 3 billion and 30 billion

annotations.note: An applied numerical example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Rounding to nearest order of magnitude

anchor.snippet: one rounds its logarithm to the nearest integer

annotations.note: Mathlib's `round` (rounding to the nearest integer) and `Real.logb` exist, but the composite procedure is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Rounding-up threshold (sqrt of ten)

anchor.snippet: requires rounding up to the next power of ten when the multiplier is greater than the square root of ten

annotations.note: The √10 rounding threshold for power-of-ten rounding is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nearest order of magnitude of 1.7e8

anchor.snippet: the nearest order of magnitude for 1.7 × 10 8 is 8

annotations.note: A specific numerical example, not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Zeroth order approximation

anchor.snippet: is sometimes also called a zeroth order approximation

annotations.note: A terminological remark with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude relative to reference value

anchor.snippet: An order of magnitude is an approximation of the logarithm of a value relative to some contextually understood reference value

annotations.note: `Real.logb` formalizes the logarithm relative to an arbitrary base, generalizing the underlying notion but not 'order of magnitude' as such.

annotations.label: Digits minus one for base 10

anchor.snippet: the order of magnitude can be understood as the number of digits minus one in the base-10 representation of the value

annotations.note: `Nat.length_digits` gives (b.digits n).length = b.log n + 1 for any base, i.e. log = digits − 1, generalizing the base-10 claim.

annotations.label: Astronomical magnitude scale

anchor.snippet: each increasing level is brighter by a factor of

annotations.note: The astronomical magnitude scale is a physics convention with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Five magnitudes brighter

anchor.snippet: a level being 5 magnitudes brighter than another

annotations.note: A domain-specific numerical example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Logarithmic scale of magnitudes

anchor.snippet: This series of magnitudes forms a logarithmic scale with a base of

annotations.note: A statement about the astronomical magnitude logarithmic scale, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of magnitude in number names

anchor.snippet: the order of magnitude is included in the number name in this example

annotations.note: A linguistic/naming observation with no formal mathematical content in Mathlib.
